w:document.mc:Ignorable: w14 w15 w16se w16cid w16 w16cex w16sdtdh w16sdtfl w16du wp14

w:r.w:t: BigWatt Digital Upsized Gas-Powered Data Center Sites
Air Permit Submissions, Decarbonization Deliverables, and Brick Full-Service Execution Plan
Prepared as a presentation-ready permitting workplan positioning Brick as the full air-
water-
permitting +
energy supply/optimization +
decarbonization execution partner, including workstreams that may otherwise be led by ASG or similar consultants.
Executive Positioning

BigWatt's upsized sites face an air-permitting problem driven by Potential to Emit (PTE), modeled ambient impacts, enforceable operating limits, public scrutiny, and the ability to show that onsite generation can operate within a defensible emissions envelope. Brick should not be positioned only as a software efficiency add-on. Brick should be positioned as the single technical owner for the air-
water-
permit submission package and the post-issuance compliance operating system.

Brick's expanded role: prepare or directly manage all air-permit deliverables; generate the PTE, BACT/LAER, dispersion modeling, monitoring, recordkeeping, and decarbonization packages; translate permit limits into live operational controls; and provide the software layer that proves compliance after COD.
Critical Regulatory Correction
Subpart KKKK applies to new, modified, or reconstructed stationary combustion turbines under 40 CFR Part 60. For modern gas turbines at BigWatt sites, this is the core NSPS rule set.

Subpart YYYY is not the primary backup-engine rule. In this context, Subpart YYYY is a 40 CFR Part 63 NESHAP for stationary combustion turbines at major HAP sources. Backup reciprocating engines are typically evaluated under 40 CFR Part 60 Subpart IIII for compression-ignition engines, Subpart JJJJ for spark-ignition engines, and 40 CFR Part 63 Subpart ZZZZ for RICE NESHAP applicability.
Air Permit Package BigWatt Must Deliver
Submission / Report
What permitting body needs
Brick deliverable BigWatt can present
Priority
1. Air Construction Permit Application Forms
State/local construction permit forms with facility identity, source description, requested permit action, source classification, controls, emissions units, and compliance certifications.

Brick Permit Application Binder: completed application forms, facility narrative, emissions-unit schedule, requested synthetic minor/major permit strategy if applicable, and permit-condition request language.
Immediate
2. Project Description + Site Development Narrative
Clear description of upsized data center campus, onsite generation concept, fuel supply, turbines, emergency/black-start assets, cooling systems, battery/thermal assets, and operating philosophy.
Brick Site + Source Narrative: permitting-grade text, process flow diagrams, equipment hierarchy, emissions-unit IDs, stack IDs, operating scenarios, and site expansion logic.
Immediate
3. Equipment Schedule / Source List
Every emissions source must be listed: turbines, HRSG if any, duct burners, emergency engines, fire pumps, boilers, fuel heaters, tanks, fugitive sources, and construction equipment if required.
Brick Emissions Unit Register: controlled source inventory with manufacturer, model, rating, heat input, fuel, stack parameters, control device, monitoring points, and permit limit linkage.
Immediate
4. Regulatory Applicability Determination
Determination of PSD/NSR/NNSR, Title V, NSPS KKKK/KKKKa if relevant, NESHAP YYYY, RICE IIII/JJJJ/ZZZZ, state toxics, GHG, and local rules.
Brick Applicability Memo: rule-by-rule applicability matrix and decision tree. Includes threshold calculations and source classification logic for the upsized site.
Immediate
5. Potential to Emit (PTE) Analysis
Maximum emissions capability before enforceable limits. PTE determines whether site is major, PSD/NNSR applicability, Title V, HAP major status, and modeling burden.
Brick PTE Workbook: uncontrolled, controlled, and proposed enforceable PTE by pollutant and emissions unit; scenario-based hourly, daily, monthly, annual calculations.
Immediate
6. Controlled Emissions Inventory
Annual and short-term emissions estimates for NOx, CO, VOC, PM/PM10/PM2.5, SO2, CO2e, HAPs, formaldehyde, and startup/shutdown events.
Brick Controlled Emissions Workbook: OEM curves + EPA factors + fuel specs + load profiles + control efficiencies + startup/shutdown factors.
Immediate
7. Operating Scenario Matrix
Permitting body needs to understand
normal operation, peak ambient operation, startup/shutdown, commissioning, emergency operation, maintenance, and upset/malfunction assumptions.
Brick Operating Scenario Matrix:
mode-by-mode dispatch profile with runtime hours, loads, turbine starts, battery use, thermal shifting, and emissions impact.
Immediate
8. BACT Analysis for PSD
If PSD major, applicant must show top-down Best Available Control Technology for each regulated pollutant and affected source.
Brick BACT Report: SCR/oxidation catalyst/dry-low-NOx/combustion controls, vendor guarantees, feasibility, cost-effectiveness, collateral impacts, proposed limits.
High
9. LAER + Offsets if Nonattainment NSR
If located in nonattainment area and thresholds are exceeded, Lowest Achievable Emission Rate and emission reduction credits may be required.
Brick LAER/Offset Strategy: LAER alternatives, offset requirement estimate, ERC strategy, and operating-limit alternatives to avoid NNSR where possible.
High / site-specific
10. Air Dispersion Modeling Protocol
Regulator often requires modeling plan before final modeling: sources, stack parameters, terrain, met data, receptors, building downwash, pollutants, averaging periods.
Brick Modeling Protocol: AERMOD-ready source inventory, receptor grid, stack data, BPIP/downwash inputs, meteorological data selection, background concentration plan.
High
11. Air Dispersion Modeling Report
Demonstrate modeled concentrations do not cause or contribute to NAAQS/PSD increment exceedances or unacceptable toxic impacts.
Brick AERMOD Report Package: model files, inputs, results tables, exceedance analyses, maps, sensitive receptor analysis, and mitigation options.
High
12. Preconstruction Ambient Air / Background Analysis
Permitting body may require background concentration data, monitoring justification, or preconstruction monitoring waiver analysis.
Brick Ambient Data Memo: monitor selection, representativeness, background values, temporal pairing, and waiver/monitoring strategy.
High
13. Additional Impact Analysis
PSD applications can require additional impacts: growth, soils, vegetation, visibility, Class I area screening if relevant.
Brick Additional Impacts Report: growth/employment/load impacts, vegetation/soil screening, visibility/Class I applicability, community sensitivity map.
Medium / PSD
14. GHG / Decarbonization Analysis
Even when GHG alone does not trigger PSD, GHG analysis, energy efficiency, and carbon strategy are increasingly important for approval and stakeholder defense.
Brick Decarbonization + GHG Report: CO2e inventory, heat-rate optimization, cooling-load reduction, battery/thermal dispatch, renewable/RNG/hydrogen optionality, avoided emissions.
High
15. Monitoring, Testing, and Compliance Plan
Regulators require enforceable monitoring: CEMS/PEMS/CPMS, stack tests, fuel monitoring, turbine operating parameters, SCR ammonia slip if applicable.
Brick Monitoring Plan: CEMS/PEMS architecture, data acquisition, sensor list, emissions proxy, data validation, QA/QC, stack test schedule, dashboard/reporting format.
High
16. Recordkeeping and Reporting Plan
Permit must define records retained and periodic reports submitted: runtime, fuel, emissions, deviations, maintenance, startups, shutdowns, tests.
Brick Compliance Data Room: automated logs, permit-limit tracker, deviation workflow, monthly/quarterly/semiannual reports, Title V-ready certification package.
High
17. Public Notice / Hearing Support Package
Large gas-powered data centers may face public scrutiny. Technical defensibility and decarb story matter.
Brick Public Support Binder: plain-English emissions explanation, decarb plan, avoided emissions, water/energy narrative, Q&A, response-to-comments technical appendix.
Medium / High
18. Draft Permit Condition Matrix
Applicant should propose practical limits and conditions to avoid unworkable permit language.
Brick Permit Condition Matrix: proposed limits, measurement method, data source, responsible system, compliance demonstration, and operational feasibility check.
Immediate
19. Post-Issuance Compliance Implementation
After permit grant, the facility must operate within conditions and produce records during inspections.
Brick Compliance OS: operational controls that enforce runtime, dispatch, emission-rate, fuel, startup/shutdown, and reporting constraints.
Post-permit but design now
How Brick Covers the ASG-Style Scope End-to-End

For BigWatt, Brick can own the full package while still collaborating with ASG if BigWatt wants ASG involved. The messaging should be: Brick is not dependent on ASG to define the permit case; Brick can produce the technical artifacts, manage the consultant workflow, interface with permitting counsel, and convert approved permit limits into operational software controls.
ASG-type workstream
Brick full-service coverage
Site development / pad-ready strategy
Brick creates the air-permit-ready basis of design, siting constraints register, stack/source locations, load and generation assumptions, and VDR-ready permit artifacts.
Permitting strategy
Brick prepares PSD/NNSR/Title V/synthetic-minor strategy, rule applicability memo, PTE scenarios, and pre-application meeting materials.
Technical filings
Brick produces application forms, emissions calculations, BACT/LAER analyses, modeling protocol, AERMOD package, GHG/decarb report, monitoring plan, and public-comment package.
Transaction readiness / VDR
Brick creates lender/buyer diligence folders: permit status tracker, regulatory risk register, emissions model, permit condition matrix, capex/opex impacts, decarb roadmap.
Post-permit compliance
Brick implements runtime, fuel, startup/shutdown, turbine dispatch, battery dispatch, and cooling optimization controls that generate compliance records automatically.
Brick Decarbonization Levers Tied Directly to Permit Submissions
Permit objective
Brick technical method
Resulting submission
Reduce PTE or proposed enforceable PTE
Use smaller guaranteed load envelope, lower cooling load, enforceable annual runtime caps, and dispatch constraints.
PTE workbook; operating limit proposal; permit condition matrix
Reduce NOx and CO modeled emissions
Optimize turbine load bands, reduce starts, implement SCR/oxidation catalyst assumptions, reduce auxiliary cooling load.
BACT report; controlled emissions workbook; startup/shutdown memo
Improve AERMOD outcomes
Lower hourly emission rates, optimize stack parameters, source locations, building downwash assumptions, and load scenarios.
Modeling protocol; AERMOD report; mitigation memo
Reduce public opposition
Quantify avoided fuel burn, avoided CO2e, lower PUE, lower water/energy waste, and transparent compliance monitoring.
Public support binder; GHG/decarbonization report
Make permit limits enforceable
Translate permit constraints into live controls, alarms, automated logs, and reports.
Monitoring plan; Compliance OS; Title V-ready reporting package
Immediate 30-Day Workplan for BigWatt
Timing
Workstream
Concrete output
Days 1-3
Kickoff + data request
Collect turbine models, site layout, MW capacity, fuel specs, cooling design, battery/thermal assets, operating assumptions, ambient data, stack info, and upsizing scope.
Days 4-7
Regulatory + PTE screen
Determine PSD/NNSR/Title V/HAP major triggers; identify whether synthetic-minor / enforceable limits can avoid major review.
Days 8-14
Emissions model + permit strategy
Build PTE/controlled emissions workbook; draft operating scenario matrix; define minimum decarbonization and controls needed to reduce permit risk.
Days 15-21
BACT/LAER + modeling protocol
Prepare top-down control technology analysis; prepare AERMOD protocol and source/receptor setup; identify mitigations before formal filing.
Days 22-30
Pre-application package
Deliver pre-application meeting deck, permit application skeleton, source list, PTE workbook, draft permit limits, compliance plan, and regulator Q&A.
Data Request BigWatt Should Send Brick Immediately
Turbine make/model, quantity, MW rating, heat input, heat rate curve, fuel type, guaranteed NOx/CO/VOC/PM/SO2/CO2e/HAP emission rates, startup/shutdown data, and control device specs.
Backup/black-start engine make/model, horsepower/kW, emergency vs non-emergency use, fuel, operating hours, stack data, and applicable certifications.
Site plan, plot plan, building dimensions, stack locations, stack heights/diameters, exhaust temperature/velocity, property boundary, nearby receptors, and terrain constraints.
Data center load profile: IT MW by phase, cooling design, PUE target, chiller/CDU/CRAH/CRAC equipment, pumps/fans/towers, thermal storage, battery systems, and operating modes.
Upsizing delta: original permitted or contemplated MW vs upsized MW; construction phasing; desired COD; targeted state/county/local jurisdiction.
Prior correspondence with permitting body, ASG work product, utility/gas constraints, public opposition risk, environmental justice screening, and local air-quality status if available.
Recommend
ations

Brick can cover the full air-permit technical package and the decarbonization operating plan for BigWatt upsized sites. We can prepare the emissions inventory, PTE analysis, BACT/LAER strategy, dispersion modeling package, compliance monitoring plan, public-response materials, and permit-condition matrix, then operationalize the permit through software controls that reduce turbine runtime, cooling load, fuel burn, startup/shutdown events, and emissions intensity.
Sources / Regulatory References
EPA NSR/PSD program overview: https://www.epa.gov/nsr
40 CFR Part 60 Subpart KKKK - stationary combustion turbines: https://www.ecfr.gov/current/title-40/chapter-I/subchapter-C/part-60/subpart-KKKK
40 CFR Part 63 Subpart YYYY - stationary combustion turbines NESHAP: https://www.ecfr.gov/current/title-40/chapter-I/subchapter-C/part-63/subpart-YYYY
40 CFR Part 60 Subpart IIII - stationary compression ignition engines: https://www.ecfr.gov/current/title-40/chapter-I/subchapter-C/part-60/subpart-IIII
40 CFR Part 60 Subpart JJJJ - stationary spark ignition engines: https://www.ecfr.gov/current/title-40/chapter-I/subchapter-C/part-60/subpart-JJJJ
EPA AERMOD / preferred dispersion models: https://www.epa.gov/scram/air-quality-dispersion-modeling-preferred-and-recommended-models
ASG site development services: https://asg-team.com/
